data.title: Buy And Hold These Ten Cheap Lithium Juniors Ready For A Potential Lithium Recovery From 2026 Onwards
data.description:
The lithium market is still experiencing a severe bear market with lithium prices well below incentive prices for many junior miners & producers. This results in many new projects being delayed and many existing project expansions also being delayed.

Meanwhile plugin EV sales (up ~25% in 2024) and battery energy stationary storage ("BESS") sales (up 53% in 2024) are still booming; which if it continues, would lead to a large tidal wave of lithium demand ahead and potentially large lithium deficits starting later this decade.

China lithium carbonate spot price 5 year chart - CNY 76,150 (~USD 10,460) (source)

A lithium demand tidal wave is potentially coming later this decade

Looking at the Trend Investing lithium demand v supply model it takes just one major project to be delayed to have a major impact on the results. For example, if Zijin Mining [SSE:601899] (OTCPK:ZIJMF) choose to further delay their 3Q Project ("Tres Quebradas") in Argentina (it has been delayed once already) it brings the lithium market back into balance.

We know new lithium projects have a very long history of being delayed, usually by 2-3 years. Zijin Mining is a copper and gold producer, so lithium is not yet important to them and a two year delay is not really significant for them. We could also say the same for Rio Tinto (RIO) who are in the process of acquiring Arcadium Lithium.

Zijin postponed the startup of projects in Argentina and Tibet - January 2025 (source)

As shown below the lithium market would quickly move to be roughly balanced in 2026-2028 and then move to massively growing deficits from 2029+ unless new supply is rapidly secured.

Trend Investing lithium demand v supply model assuming Zijin's 3Q Project is delayed by 2 years

Note: The numbers show the market balance, surplus or in brackets deficit.

The take away here is that it really will only take one or two new projects to be delayed for the lithium market to balance and more significantly lead to huge deficits later this decade.

Many investors have no idea the huge lithium demand wave that potentially lies ahead. Just look at the demand growth numbers in our model below for 2025 onwards (green shaded background). In 2030 lithium demand will potentially increase in just one year by almost the entire size of the industry in 2024.

Note: These are model numbers and are therefore only a forecast; however up until now Trend Investing's forecasts on EV sales and lithium demand have been right on the mark.

Note: McKinsey and the Global Battery Alliance forecast a 27% compound annual growth rate ("CAGR") on demand for lithium-ion batteries, reaching 4,700 GWh by 2030 (source page 11). At 0.85kg Li/kWh 4,700 GWh by 2030 equates to 3.995 million tonnes lithium demand by 2030 (by end 2029). Our model above has the end 2029 lithium total demand at 3.853 million tonnes (a similar result).

If you believe that EV and BESS adoption will continue to grow rapidly each year then the above numbers give a great guide as to the massive demand and potential deficits that lie ahead.

This is the opportunity for nimble junior lithium miners that can fill a potential tight supply market from 2026 and a huge potential supply gap from 2029 onwards.

Ten cheap lithium juniors ready for a potential lithium recovery from 2026 onwards

Below is a table of the cheap 'quality' lithium juniors with potential to be near term producers this decade. All have good lithium projects in relatively safe jurisdictions (Australia, Canada, Brazil, Argentina).

Potential production start Market cap (US$m) stock price
Atlas Lithium (ATLX) ~Late 2025 92 US$5.97
Galan Lithium [ASX:GLN] (OTCPK:GLNLF) H2, 2025- H1, 2026 58 A$0.12
Lithium Ionic Corp. [TSXV:LTH] (OTCQX:LTHCF) ~H2, 2026 112 C$1.00
Critical Elements Lithium [TSXV:CRE] (OTCQX:CRECF) ~2027 85 C$0.55
Frontier Lithium [TSXV:FL] (OTCQX:LITOF) ~H1, 2027 84 C$0.53
Winsome Resources [ASX:WR1] (OTCQB:WRSLF) ~2028 44 A$0.31
Patriot Battery Metals [TSX:PMET] [ASX:PMT] (OTCQX:PMETF) ~2029 304 C$2.74
Global Lithium Resources [ASX:GL1](OTCPK:GBLRF) ~2030 33 A$0.20
Lithium South Development Corp. [TSXV:LIS] (OTCQB:LISMF)/POSCO ~2030 10 C$0.13
Argentina Lithium & Energy [TSXV:LIT] ~2030+ 10 C$0.11

Note: The links above to the estimated production date start will mostly direct you to the latest 'company presentations'. These dates are subject to change and will depend upon various milestones being achieved, notably in most cases project funding.

There are other lithium juniors to consider such as Volt Lithium [CVE:VLT] (OTCQB:VLTLF), Atlantic Lithium [LSE:ALL] [ASX:A11] (OTCQX:ALLIF), Standard Lithium [TSXV:SLI] (SLI), ioneer Ltd [ASX:INR] [GR:4G1] (IONR), Lithium Americas [TSX:LAC] (LAC), Sibanye Stillwater [JSE:SSW] (SBSW), Vulcan Energy Resources [ASX:VUL] (OTCPK:VULNF), Savannah Resources [LSE:SAV] [GR:SAV] (OTCPK:SAVNF), European Lithium Ltd [ASX:EUR] (OTCQB:EULIF)/ Critical Metals Corp (CRML), Delta Lithium [ASX:DLI], Q2 Metals Corp. [TSXV:QTWO] (QTWO), Avalon Advanced Materials [TSX:AVL] (OTCQX:AVLNF), European Metals Holdings [ASX:EMH] [AIM:EMH] [GR:E861] (OTCQX:EMHLF) (OTCQX:EMHXY), E3 Lithium Ltd. [TSXV:ETL] [FSE:OW3] (OTCQX:EEMMF), Century Lithium [TSXV:LCE] (OTCQX:CYDVF), Nevada Lithium Corp. [TSXV:NVLH] [FSE:87K](OTCQB:NVLHF), Snow Lake Lithium (LITM), Green Technology Metals [ASX: GT1], Wildcat Resources [ASX:WC8], Lake Resources NL [ASX:LKE] [GR:LK1] (OTCQB:LLKKF), American Lithium Corp. [TSXV:LI] [FSE:5LA1] (OTC:AMLIF), Wealth Minerals [TSXV:WML] [GR:EJZN] (OTCQB:WMLLF), and St George Mining Ltd. [ASX:SGQ] [GR:SOG] etc that can be considered.

Please see our "Lithium junior monthly news" for a more complete summary and latest news for each company. It should be noted also that Rio Tinto [ASX:RIO] [LN:RIO] (RIO) is making big moves into the lithium sector.

Risks

  • The assumptions in our model may not come true.
  • Lithium supply could continue to exceed demand, thereby limiting the opportunity for lithium juniors.
  • Mining risks - Exploration risks, funding risks, permitting risks, development and production risks.
  • Sovereign risk - Low in Canada and Australia. Moderate in Brazil and Argentina.
  • Company risks - Management, debt, liquidity, currency risks.
  • Stock market risks - Dilution, lack of liquidity (best to buy on local exchange), market sentiment.

Note: Junior miners are inherently risky due to a combination of many of the risks above.

Further reading

  • Jan. 2025 - Global electric vehicle sales up 25% in record 2024
  • Jan. 2025 - Global BESS deployments soared 53% in 2024
  • Lithium Junior Miners News For The Month Of January 2025

Fastmarkets spodumene price forecast 2024-2034 - Prices forecast to improve from 2026 onwards (source)

Conclusion

The lithium junior miners (ie: those not yet in production) have had a torrid past 2.5 years as lithium prices collapsed and sentiment deteriorated towards the sector. This has resulted in some extremely low valuations for most lithium juniors.

Looking ahead to the next few years there is a strong possibility that new lithium projects or expansions get delayed. Assuming very strong lithium demand continues then we may get a more balanced lithium market from 2026 to 2028 and potentially large deficits from 2029 onwards. Any demand surge from the new ~US$28,00 Tesla (TSLA) Model Q or other cheaper EVs in the West may bring forward these deficits to occur sooner rather than later.

This sets up very nicely for patient investors willing to buy and hold the quality lithium juniors for the next 5-7 years plus. Yes some will fail, but those that can succeed have potential for very large returns.

Please read the risks section.

As usual all comments are welcome.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MAXI: Long Bitcoin With A 32% Yield
data.description:
Introduction

In my quest to build the perfect portfolio, I have found a desire to include Bitcoin (BTC-USD) exposure, at least in a very small amount. I most recently discussed Bitcoin's place in a portfolio in an article published in December. That article covers my multi-asset portfolio that uses leverage to enhance returns. It was a fun write, and I recommend taking a look if you're curious about how I justify Bitcoin's inclusion.

In the pursuit of the "perfect" allocation to Bitcoin, I've stumbled upon several funds that utilize interesting strategies, leveraging the availability of Bitcoin derivatives to create income-producing funds that also offer long exposure to Bitcoin itself.

The one that captivated me the most, and that I employ in that portfolio, is the Simplify Bitcoin Strategy PLUS Income ETF (NASDAQ:MAXI).

Over its lifetime, it has performed well, producing significant yield. Its price return since its launch in Q4 2022 is nearly 115%, but its total return (including dividends paid, but not reinvested) is over 310%.

While this doesn't compare to Bitcoin itself, necessarily, the income paid to investors during that time could be worthwhile to many investors.

Consider that its trailing yield is currently almost 32%, and it's fairly impressive to see that it's kept up with long-only exposure.

Strategy

The fund works off of several moving parts, which may seem complex at first, but are fairly straightforward as individual components.

Here is how Simplify explains it:

The Simplify Bitcoin Strategy PLUS Income ETF (NASDAQ:MAXI) seeks capital gains through an actively managed Bitcoin strategy. A risk-managed options overlay is added to provide income.The Bitcoin exposure will vary between 50% to 200% of the fund’s net assets, based on a proprietary technical model. Exposure may be gained through various instruments, including Bitcoin ETFs, futures, options, or swaps.The options income strategy will focus on selling put spreads on a variety of instruments, including equity indices as well as bond and commodity ETFs.**The fund does not invest in bitcoin directly.

Piece One: Treasuries As Collateral

The fund first buys treasuries, with effectively 100% of its original NAV. These treasuries are used as collateral to buy Bitcoin exposure through futures, as well as to sell options spreads.

This makes the base of the fund look like this:

Currently, about 90% of assets are tied up in T-Bills.

Options and futures are capital efficient, however, meaning that they can be held using existing assets as collateral. This means that the fund maintains its 90-100% allocation to T-Bills no matter what else is does in the fund, since the rest of its operations are all derivative-based.

Having the allocation to MAXI in my portfolio contribute to my allocations to Bitcoin, T-Bills, and an options-income strategy was very enticing, and it allows me to do more with less; instead of spreading my allotted allocation across three ETFs doing these.

Piece Two: Options Spreads

Simplify has several partnerships with hedge funds and other investments advisors, and it's unclear if their options-selling algorithm is entirely in-house or not, but they employ a machine to trade options spreads. They explain it this way:

The risk-managed option overlay sells put spreads on a variety of instruments to generate income. This predominantly includes spreads on equity indices as well as on fixed income, commodity, volatility, and currency ETFs and exchange traded products (ETPs), and on Bitcoin futures.

This means that their short exposure will be varied, and not tied directly to Bitcoin. That is a positive thing for MAXI investors as it means that the income is not directly tied to the capping of upside on Bitcoin itself, but rather volatility across other instruments that may not correlate with Bitcoin's returns. This is unlike other Bitcoin income funds that cap the upside of Bitcoin in order to generate its income. MAXI, on the other hand, currently has no options spreads on Bitcoin itself, although they do have positions open against Microstrategy (MSTR), which trades in great sympathy with Bitcoin.

Their options strategy is to open put spreads on various instruments, to take advantage of mispricing opportunities that arise in these assets. It's a black box model, so we can't predict or forecast how the fund will perform on an income front, but here are its total holdings if it helps anybody make sense of their strategy.

Note that the image above also includes a 7.31% allocation to IBIT calls, which are technically options, but are not part of the options-selling program and are instead part of the next component, the long Bitcoin exposure.

Piece Three: Bitcoin Exposure

Keeping in mind that IBIT call allocation enhances the exposure to Bitcoin, the fund also keeps a more "traditional" Bitcoin exposure tool, CME futures. Commodities futures are typically what comes to mind when funds use the words "Bitcoin strategy," as it is not technically investing in "physical" Bitcoin. Instead, it's invested in synthetic Bitcoin, which are backed by cash transactions and not the delivery of actual Bitcoins.

This is not a problem for me, but may be for some folks who want "pure" exposure to Bitcoin without the worry of having to roll futures contracts and deal with the issues that come along with that, namely "carry," or the cost paid for the leverage obtained.

That carry is expensive, and costs MAXI over 500bp in fees annually.

Dynamic Exposure to Bitcoin

The keen-eyed among you may have noticed that the total exposure to Bitcoin was about 57% of NAV currently. This is intentionally less than 100%. Let's call back to the original explainer of this strategy for a reminder:

The Bitcoin exposure will vary between 50% to 200% of the fund’s net assets, based on a proprietary technical model.

Back on December 31st, 2024, Simplify reported MAXI's holdings at over 100% Bitcoin exposure. They have since scaled back, based on their technical readings of the market.

This model is designed to reduce the volatility from holding Bitcoin. Does it work? In the chart below, we can see that MAXI tends to be more volatile than a "pure" Bitcoin fund like IBIT. This is likely due to the volatility from the options, as well as over-exposure to Bitcoin during drawdowns. The volatility diverged dramatically in December last year, but MAXI's is quickly falling back in line.

Because of the other moving parts of the fund, it's unclear if MAXI has the ability to be less volatile than just owning Bitcoin, since some of these fluctuations could be from the put spreads. This is the most major red flag I see with this fund, is that it is not actually less volatile than owning Bitcoin outright.

This strategy has promise, however, as its performance has kept up with Bitcoin over time despite its crazy high yield, over 30%.

Suitability

MAXI is an interesting fund, and one that I believe has great potential as a high-yield way to own Bitcoin, with performance similar to the asset, while still producing 30%+ in income.

There are some red flags, however, such as the high ER from the use of leverage, and the leverage itself. Some investors may be more comfortable owning funds with less "black box" components, or less active management. Volatility is also very high, even higher than Bitcoin outright at times due to the income mechanism.

To this end, I am recommending that investors very carefully consider MAXI when including it in a portfolio. Aggressive investors could consider up to a 3% allocation in a diversified portfolio, and moderate investors could consider up to a 1% allocation. Conservative investors are cautioned to stay away from cryptocurrencies entirely, let alone Bitcoin funds that sell options like MAXI.

In my own portfolio, it holds a 2.5% allocation. You can check that out here.

Conclusion

Ultimately, the Simplify Bitcoin Strategy PLUS Income ETF (MAXI) has impressed me. While it has black box components that make it difficult to predict and forecast, these also contribute to its potential for outperformance. The fund itself has kept up with Bitcoin's performance while distributing a trailing return of 30%+ in income.

This fund may be very appealing to income investors, especially those interested in having Bitcoin exposure. The strategy barbells Bitcoin with T-Bills and adds an aggressive options overlay that does not materially decrease Bitcoin exposure. This means that compared to other Bitcoin income funds, MAXI should receive more of Bitcoin's upside, as it does not cap it for its income returns. In exchange, MAXI can be volatile in ways that Bitcoin isn't, as it sells options on commodities and equities.

Investors should heed caution when considering MAXI, and allocate capital appropriately for their risk tolerance, but it earns a buy rating from me as someone who was previously fairly coin-shy.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: AbbVie Vs. Johnson & Johnson: Graham Would Prefer The Consumer Healthcare Giant
data.description:
ABBV and JNJ stock: Previous thesis and Q4 earnings

My last analyses on Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ) and AbbVie (NYSE:ABBV) were both published before the release of their earnings. Furthermore, both articles placed an emphasis on dividends. More specifically, my last article on JNJ was titled “Dividend Roundup: Why I Prefer Johnson & Johnson Over Pfizer” and was published by Seeking Alpha on 1.8.2025. The article rated JNJ as a Strong Buy based on the following considerations:

I want to caution investors to look beyond yield (and P/E too) and examine other aspects, especially those often overlooked by dividend investors. JNJ offers better growth, a stronger track record, and also a safer payout.

As for ABBV, my last work was titled “AbbVie And Pfizer: Q3 Dividend Checkup Post-Earnings” and was published by Seeking Alpha on 11.11.2024. As the title already hinted, that article’s goal was to perform a holistic checkup of ABBV’s dividends. I concluded with a Hold rating for ABBV after considering its dividend safety, PEGY ratio (P/E growth yield ratio), share repurchase program and also inventory management.

Since then, there have been a few important new catalysts evolving around both stocks, with the most important one being earnings. For readers unfamiliar with the numbers, the following two charts provide a summary. Overall, I think ABBV delivered a stronger Q4 and JNJ is facing some more pronounced unevenness (I will elaborate more on this in the risk section later). Under this backdrop, my goal of this article is to analyze their latest earnings and also the latest earnings forecast through the framework Ben Graham developed for picking defensive stocks. As you will see in the remainder of this article, my results show that JNJ is still a better defensive stock based on this framework despite the more mixed Q4 financials. The updates provided in their Q4 ER (together with the stock price changes) have also led to me adjust JNJ’s rating to buy from my early strong buy rating while maintaining my hold rating on ABBV.

ABBV, JNJ, and Benjamin Graham’s Checklist

In his book, The Intelligent Investor, Ben Graham described a framework for selecting defensive stocks. This framework is detailed in my earlier article and the gist is summarized below. I feel this framework is very fitting given ABBV and JNJ’s leading status in a stable sector. And I also feel that the framework provides a better context to filter out the near-term fluctuations as those reflected in their Q4 ER just mentioned above.

  1. Is the company large, prominent, and conservatively financed? The specific metrics to look for are stable financial strength, consistent capital structure, and a long record of continuous dividend payments.
  2. Especially the dividend record. Graham emphasized countless times the importance of dividend records – for good reason. In his own words, he thinks “a record of continuous dividend payments for the last 20 years or more is an important plus factor in the company’s quality rating”.
  3. Has the company demonstrated an adequate level of earnings growth in the past? For defensive investors, growth is not the key and “adequate” is enough. In Graham’s mind, a minimum increase of at least one third in per-share earnings in the past 10 years is adequate enough.
  4. Finally, are the valuation multiples moderate? As a value investor to the core, he also recommended a series of methods for investors to gauge the price they should pay. And also, being fully aware of the uncertainties in his own method, he emphasized that you should always leave a safe margin of safety.

ABBV and JNJ: Both are excellent defensive stocks

To start, both ABBV and JNJ fit very nicely to the first two requirements in Graham’s framework. Both are excellent defensive stocks featuring strong financial strength, a long record of dividend payouts and also dividend growth (both are dividend champions), and a leading position in a stable section.

ABBV is an integrated biopharmaceutical company engaged in the development, manufacture, commercialization and sale of innovative medicines and therapies worldwide. Its portfolio includes a broad range of therapeutic areas such as immunology, hematologic oncology, aesthetics, neuroscience, eye care, etc. Similarly, JNJ is also engaged and well established in the research and development, manufacture, and sale of a broad range of products in the healthcare field. Through its two main business segments (Innovative Medicine and Medical Devices), it provides healthcare products to a broad customer base in areas encompassing antipsychotic, contraceptive, dermatology, orthopedic, surgery, eye health fields, etc.

Although at a quantitative level, my view is that JNJ exhibits a stronger balance sheet than ABBV judging by a range of metrics reported from the Q4 ER. As you can see from the following chart, JNJ holds significantly more cash than ABBV both in total amount and also on a per share basis. JNJ holds a total cash position of $24.52B currently, translating into more than $10 per share, compared to ABBV's $5.56B (or $3.13 per share). JNJ's total debt is considerably lower too, again both in absolute and relative terms. In absolute dollar amount, ABBV carries a heavier debt load of $68.25B, compared to JNJ's total debt of $37.83B. In terms of their debt-to-equity ratios, ABBV's ratio is about 2,028%, whereas JNJ's ratio is about 53%. In terms of the ratio between their long-term debt and total capital, ABBV’s ratio of 85% is also substantially higher than JNJ’s 29%.

ABBV and JNJ stock: Earnings growth

In terms of growth, Graham’s requirement is relatively low. To wit, his requirement is an increase of at least one third in earnings over a period of 10 years. These figures translate into an average annual growth rate of 2.9% only.

Both JNJ and ABBV have demonstrated a growth far exceeding this requirement in the past. Here, I will use their dividend growth to assess their earnings growth. As dividend champions, I consider their dividends a better metric for their owners’ earnings than accounting EPS. As you can see from the following chart, ABBV's five-year compound annual growth rate for dividends is 7.46%, and JNJ's CAGR is 5.47%. In the past three years, ABBV and JNJ have been maintaining a similar CAGR of 5.81% and 5.37%, respectively and both exceeding Graham’s requirement by almost a factor of 2.

ABBV and JNJ’s valuation

The last item on Graham’s list is valuation. Here, I will rely on the now-called Graham P/E to compare the valuations for both ABBV and JNJ:

In his book, Graham recommended a series of methods for investors to gauge the price they should pay. Here we will examine the so-called Graham P/E. Graham recommended the P/E for a defensive stock should be around 8.5 plus twice the expected annual growth rate. Hence, in his mind, a business that completely stagnates should be worth about 8.5x P/E.

In ABBV’s case, the stock is trading at an FWD FY1 P/E of 16x as you can see from the table below. If I use its dividend growth rate of 5.81% in the past three years to approximate its future earnings growth rate, then its Graham P/E would be around 20x (5.81x2 + 8.5). Thus, its current market P/E is discounted from the Graham P/E by about 20%, providing a sizable margin of safety.

In JNJ’s case, the stock is trading at an FWD FY1 P/E of 14.7x. Again, if I assume its dividend growth rate of 5.37% in the past three years to be a good approximation of its forward earnings growth rate, then its Graham P/E would be around 19.2x (5.37x2 + 8.5). Thus, its current market P/E is discounted from the Graham P/E by about 24%, an even wider margin of safety than ABBV.

Other risks and final thoughts

In terms of downside risks, as aforementioned, I'm seeing more unevenness for JNJ than ABBV. For ABBV’s, I feel its growth story is pretty clear (not implying any degree of certainty here). Its growth story is centered on the replacement of its Humira franchise by two newcomers: SkyRizi and Rinvoq. The company has been reporting substantial impacts from biosimilars on its Humira assets. So, the risk/uncertainty here is if the newcomers can grow rapidly enough to offset the Humira decline. The developments, as reported in earnings, seem very promising. Skyrizi has now become the company’s best-selling product, and I see Rinvoq well poised to become a blockbuster too.

By comparison, JNJ has more moving parts in my assessment. First, I expect the company’s performance in the near term to be impacted by the fluctuations in COVID-19 vaccine demand and also currency impact. The company is also in the process of digesting several acquisitions. For example, it recently closed three acquisitions. On the positive side, these acquisitions helped its sales (notably for its MedTech segment). But its operating margin has felt the pressure lately due to M&A expenses and integration costs. Finally, its R&D as a percentage of sales has also been on the rise lately, creating future pressure on the bottom line.

All told, I consider all these above issues to be ultimately temporary. As stated upfront, the goal of this article is to analyze these issues through the framework of Graham, which is more geared toward the longer term and helps to filter out short-term fluctuation. Under this framework, I see both JNJ and ABBV as attractive candidates fitting Graham’s criteria for defensive stocks nicely both in term of quality and also valuation. I also see JNJ as a stronger fit due to its better financial strength and also lower valuation ratios. However, the issues above, when combined with the price rally since my last writing, did lead me to adjust its rating to buy compared to my earlier strong buy rating.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Contrarian Buy Opportunity For Those With Higher Risk Appetite
data.description:
SMCI's Oversold Status Triggers The Cheap Valuations For Those With Higher Risk Appetite

We previously covered Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) in November 2024, discussing its robust recovery prospects through the strategic partnership with Nvidia (NVDA) and recent onboarding of a new auditor, with it implying the former's FQ1'25 Business Update numbers might be reliable to a large extent.

Even then, we had maintained our Hold rating then, pending the management's upcoming FY2024 10K/ FQ1'25 10Q filing, the avoidance of delisting, and the restoration of investors' trust.

SMCI 1Y Stock Price

Since then, SMCI has already charted an impressive recovery of +54.8%, well outperforming the wider market at +2.2% and its peers to a large degree, thanks to the lifting market sentiments surrounding its potential turnaround as we enter the Q4'24 earning season.

Part of the tailwinds may be attributed to the promising AI/ data center related spending trends ahead, as similarly highlighted by Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG), and Meta (META) in their recent earnings call, with it underscoring the multi-year nature of this cloud super cycle.

The same robust numbers have also been observed in SMCI's FQ2'25 preliminary financial information with revenues of $5.65B (-5% QoQ/ +54.3% YoY) and adj EPS of $0.59 (-21.8% QoQ/ +7.2% YoY), with it underscoring why the stock has outperformed as it has.

The same has been observed in its FQ3'25 guidance, with revenues of $5.5B at the midpoint (-2.6% QoQ/ +42.8% YoY) still underscoring its ability to remain relevant during the ongoing investment cycle, albeit with an impacted adj EPS guidance of $0.54 (-8.4% QoQ/ -18.1% YoY).

For now, part of the bottom-line headwinds may be attributed to SMCI's choice to cut ASPs, as observed in the lower FQ2'25 gross margins of 11.9% (-1.4 points QoQ/ -3.6 YoY) and the FQ3'25 guidance of 12% (+0.1 points QoQ/ -3.6 YoY).

This was compared to the FY2023 levels of 18.1% (+2.7 points YoY) when they were the first movers and had massive pricing power.

This is on top of the growing number of competitors jumping into the data center cooling systems and/ or complete server rack solutions, including:

  • Modine Manufacturing Company (MOD),
  • Lennox International Inc. (LII),
  • AAON, Inc. (AAON),
  • Vertiv Holdings (VRT), and
  • Foxconn Technology (OTCPK:FXCOF),

with it underscoring why SMCI is unlikely to report similarly rich gross margins moving forward, with these low double digit numbers likely to be a new normal as they seek to generate robust growth through volume sales.

The same growth headwind has also been observed in SMCI's moderately lowered FY2025 revenue guidance to $24.25B at the midpoint (+62.3% YoY), down from the original numbers of $28B (+87.4% YoY).

This development demonstrates why the ongoing auditor issues have flowed into the mixed customer confidence and impacted sequential growth thus far.

On the other hand, it appears that the bottom-line headwinds may be temporary, given that SMCI has already offered a promising FY2026 revenue guidance of $40B (+64.9% YoY).

The company appears to be well capitalized to weather the near-term uncertainties as well, thanks to the still reasonable net debts of -$0.5B in FQ2'25, compared to -$0.2B in FQ1'25 and $0.35B of net cash in FQ2'24, significantly aided by the additional note convertible worth $700M due 2028.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons are likely why the consensus have moderately raised their forward estimates, with SMCI expected to generate an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +34.4%/ +22% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +28.9%/ +14.9%, while building upon the 5Y historical growth at +33.7%/ +66.7% and 10Y growth at +26.1%/ +32.3%, respectively, assuming that the management is able to deliver as guided.

SMCI Valuations

This reason may also be why the market has temporarily upgraded SMCI's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 18.30x, up from the November 2024 bottom of 6.24x and elevated from the 5Y mean of 15.83x, albeit still a big distance away from the peak of 43.99x observed in March 2024.

The same compelling valuations are also observed at SMCI's reasonable PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.49x, compared to the sector median of 1.85x and its thermal management/ data center infrastructure peers, including MOD at 1.08x, LII at 1.84x, AAON at 2.90x, VRT at 1.10x, and FXCOF at 1.15x, with it undeniable that the former is extremely cheap at current levels, with it offering interested investors with an improved margin of safety.

Naturally, this is assuming that the bulls continue to support SMCI's upward recovery and the company successfully submit the "fiscal year 2024 form 10-K and the first two quarters of fiscal year 2025 Form 10-Q" by February 25th, 2025, as "the Special Committee found no evidence to support the former auditor’s reasons for resignation."

So, Is SMCI Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

SMCI 2Y Stock Price

For now, SMCI appears to have charted an impressive recovery since the November 2024 bottom and February 2025 bottom, with the stock seemingly retesting the prior August 2024 resistance levels of $60s ranges.

For context, we had offered a long-term price target of $74.10 in our last article, based on the prior consensus FY2026 adj EPS estimates of $3.37 and the 1Y P/E mean of 22x.

For now, as the management delivers robust FQ2'25 performance while guiding promising FY2026 numbers, it is unsurprising that the consensus have raised their FY2026 adj EPS estimates to $3.74, with it leading to our raised long-term price target of $82.30.

Does this mean that we are upgrading the SMCI stock to a Buy here?

Yes indeed, though with a few caveats.

It goes without saying that SMCI is likely to remain highly volatile in the coming week, given the uncertainty surrounding the upcoming filing as NVDA is also set to report their FQ4'25 performance in February 26, 2025, with most of the market likely to tune in while awaiting their FY2026 guidance.

For now, we are cautiously optimistic that the king of semiconductor stocks is likely to beat estimates, thanks to the 'staggering Blackwell demand' and the Blackwell powered AI/ HPC servers expected to "reach mass production and peak shipments during the second and third quarters of 2025."

Despite the rerouted client orders to other suppliers, we believe that the data center market demand remains robust enough to accommodate multiple winners, as some of TSM's Arizona/ Japan capacity and numerous data center REITs' capacity also come online from 2025 onwards.

Even so, given the drastic increase in its short interest volume by +82.1% on a YoY basis and the lowered FY2025 revenue guidance, we urge interested readers to observe the SMCI stock price movement for a little longer and only adding upon a moderate retracement to the prior support levels of $40s ranges for an improvement margin of safety.

These reasons are also why the stock is only suitable for those with higher risk appetite, as observed in the elevated beta at 1.47x.

data.title: APA Corporation: Suriname's Promise Vs. Oil Risks - Value Trap Ahead?
data.description:
APA Corporation (NASDAQ:APA) is an independent energy company with a diverse portfolio of offshore and onshore assets across the United States, Egypt, and the North Sea. The company also holds several untapped and exploratory assets, particularly in Suriname, Alaska, and Uruguay.

APA’s business model revolves around the exploration and production of crude oil, which accounts for 70% of its revenue, natural gas (~20%), and natural gas liquids (~10%).

Over the last year, the company has been actively reshuffling its asset portfolio. This includes the divestiture of approximately $950 million in non-core permian assets and a partnership with Total Energies in Suriname. These moves are aimed at optimizing the company’s portfolio by focusing on higher-margin assets.

I came across this stock while screening low P/E ratio companies in the S&P 500. Currently, the company is trading at a forward P/E ratio of ~5.8, presenting an attractive risk-reward profile at the outset.

In this article, I take a deep dive into whether the company is an attractive value play or a value trap by analyzing each of its assets and assessing the associated risks and rewards with them.

Current asset profile

APA was founded in the 1950s as Apache Oil Corporation and has transitioned from a small U.S.-based driller to an international E&P player, now included in the S&P 500. Over the past several decades, the company has successfully navigated the cyclical nature of the oil industry while strategically increasing its production. In the 1990s, APA expanded into international markets through an acquisition in Egypt, and in 2003, it entered the North Sea market.

To understand APA, it is crucial to examine its business model and asset base. The company derives ~70% of its BoE equivalent production from its U.S. assets, primarily in the Permian Basin, where it has around 4,000+ drilling locations. The company’s breakeven price in this region is estimated at ~$45 per barrel WTI. Next, about 20% of its production comes from Egypt, where it benefits from long-term government concessions in the Western Desert and Mediterranean Sea. This region has historically been highly profitable for APA, allowing it to realize above-average returns vs US assets due to favourable gas pricing. Lastly, ~10% of the output comes from the North sea region. However, the company is facing high regulatory costs in the region and is planning to ramp down operations here by 2029.

U.S. Permian Basin

As we can see by the production estimates shared by the company for 2025, it seems that the outlook for 2025 in terms of production output is very similar to last year. Given stable output, the total revenues and hence free cash flow that is generated by the company in this region is largely dependent on the oil and gas prices in the Permian Basin.

At this point, our analysis of the revenues & free cash flows becomes very challenging and would largely depend on the macroeconomic view individual investors may have about oil and gas prices in 2025. Being a very cautious and prudent investor in this regard, I would like to go ahead with the assumption that oil and gas prices continue to stay subdued this year.

I believe in this hypothesis because any geopolitical development which eases the sanctions on Russia or Iran could also lead to a supply glut in the market. Moreover, the current production profile for Permian-related assets leads to a breakeven cost of approximately $40-60 per barrel in the region- depending on the age of asset. Compare that to the just $10 per barrel cost that a country like Saudi Arabia has. Currently OPEC has been holding back on supply during the last year but is finding it increasingly difficult to contain individual member countries. In this situation, I believe it is only a matter of time before Saudi Arabia unleashes an ocean of supply into the market.

This, coupled with a slower-than-expected recovery in China and a “Drill, Baby, Drill” policy under Trump increasing the supply of crude further in the near future, this is the perfect catalyst for increasing supply and reducing demand.

While bulls may argue that the demand for power due to artificial intelligence is going to be insatiable and will lead to a strong increase in dependency on fossil fuels such as natural gas, I believe that cycle will play out over a slightly longer time and may not materialize, at least in 2025. I say this because large tech companies would first prefer to tie up with cleaner power providers such as nuclear or renewable companies, and natural gas is expected to be largely used for grid balancing and peak power management. Moreover, the entire hypothesis of higher power demand also suddenly came into question with the Deepseek breakthrough.

Hence, in my opinion, we should ideally anticipate some reduction in revenues & operational FCF generated from the US-based assets compared to current levels - even if the production remains stable.

International Operations

Within its international operations, APA derives revenue primarily from two key assets—its profitable Egypt business and the challenging waters of the North Sea.

As far as the Egypt business is concerned, I see some stability going forward. The production output is expected to remain consistent and pricing is also likely to stay high. As per the company:

Subsequent to September 30, 2024, but prior to the date of this filing, the Company entered into a new pricing agreement for incremental gas volumes produced in Egypt, making gas exploration and development more economically competitive with oil development.

My understanding is that the Egyptian General Petroleum Corporation is likely to give APA some fixed commitments/ prices, to increase gas output, due to the increased gas demand in the country for power generation. The gas is consumed by utilities in Egypt for power generation, and can be purchased at fixed longer term contracts due to the relatively stable nature of electricity prices.

However, it is noteworthy to mention that the majority of the business done by APA in Egypt is with the government or government entities, and the country is facing extreme economic hardships. The country’s currency has been devalued multiple times last year, and the revenues from Egypt should ideally command a higher country risk premium. The company faced some challenges with receivables from the EG government, as cited in the 10-Q report:

In the past year, the Company’s receivable balancefrom the Egyptian General Petroleum Corporation (EGPC) has gradually increased as payments for the Company’s Egyptian oil and gas sales have been delayed for periods longer than historically experienced. The Company is actively engaged in discussions with the Government of Egypt to resolve the delay in EGPC payments. The Company has received payments throughout the period, and management believes that the Company will be able to collect the total balance of its receivables from this customer.

As far as the North Sea operations are concerned, management has forecasted a ~20% reduction in output from this region. However this should be offset by minor increases in other assets, as north sea contributes only ~10% to the production output of APA. Moreover, by 2029 APA expects to exit from this asset, which should be reflected in the revenues going ahead.

To conclude, I believe that in 2025, despite fluctuations in oil and gas prices, Revenue & Cash flow generated from APA’s international operations should remain largely in line with 2024 levels due to the relatively attractive nature of Egyptian contracts and the relatively small share of income from the North Sea.

Elephant in the room - Suriname

With the core business largely stable and linked to oil prices, it becomes critical to assess the optional valuation of the Suriname asset and the likely capital investment until production to ascertain the fair value of APA.

As published in multiple analyst and news articles, the Suriname asset is being developed by APA in partnership with Total with a 50-50% share in the proceeds of the asset. The asset is expected to generate around 220,000 barrels per day of output and is likely to go live by H1 2028.

The market was extremely worried that this large capital heavy asset may be a drain on the free cash flow of the company until development. The total project is currently estimated to have a capex of $10.5bn. However, as we have seen with multiple E&P projects over the last decade, costs and timeline only go up. From a cost perspective, the current agreement between Total and APA is that 87.5% of the cost up to the first $10 billion is expected to be borne by Total and the remaining 12.5% is expected to be borne by APA. For the next $5 billion, the ratio skews a bit and APA has the increased liability of ~25% of the total cost. For all costs above $15 billion, the ratio further increases and APA is expected to bear ~37.5% of the capex.

Assuming no major overruns, I estimate a cash outflow of ~$1.2 Bn over the next 4 years. These are broadly in line with the assumptions forecasted by APA management- i.e. ~250-300 million dollars per year of additional capex. This is likely to impact the FCF available to shareholders between 2025-2028, and will likely reduce the FCF yield.


Valuation

The FCF assumptions for APA are highly dependent on the global crude prices. To ensure we are taking into account a broad range of situations, I started with the unlevered cash flows for APA in the last 5 years. As you can see from the data below, 2022 (when avg. crude prices were ~$100/bbl) marks a potential ceiling for cashflows. On the other hand, 2023 & 2024 (when Avg. crude prices were ~$80/bbl) we see the avg cashflows ~$1bn. For 2025- I start with the TTM FCF as a base - when crude prices have been ~$80/bbl.

As per the management, the production is expected to slightly increase, but costs/bbl are expected to decline by ~15-18%. The cost reduction, coupled with ~15-19% lower capex may lead to an optimistic case scenario of ~30% increase in unlevered FCF. Even if we assume this relatively optimistic growth in FCF, I arrive at a fair market value of only ~$22.4/share which is almost equal to the current share price. This is partially driven by the high WACC expectation of the company, given it has a high beta of ~1.3 and the cash flows are extremely volatile.

Conclusion

Given I do not see a clear good news catalyst for the company in the near future, but multiple sources of potential bad news (e.g. increased oil supply by OPEC, removing of sanctions on Russia, slower industrial growth in China or over-run of Suriname costs) I would ideally rate the stock as a hold and wait for a better entry point.

data.title: New Listings Spring Early
data.description:
Despite snow banks and cold temps in much of Canada, property owners are getting a jump on what they hope will be strong spring demand.

New property listings leapt 11% nationwide from December to January, the largest monthly increase in almost two years.

The supply influx boosted the inventory of homes for sale in Canada to its highest level since the onset of the pandemic. But so far, it’s failed to attract the desired jump in home sales - the number of transactions dipped in the past two months, including a 3.3% decline in January.

Canada’s annual sales-to-new listings ratio is shown below since 2004. When this ratio dips below .40, market conditions favour buyers over sellers. A full-on buyer’s market has not been seen nationally since this ratio fell to .36 in November 2008. See RBC, Canadian Buyers unfazed by sellers’ early jump into the housing market.

Under the hood, the sales to new listings ratio varies significantly by metropolitan area (shown below). Toronto and Vancouver, at .33 and .37, respectively, were weaker in January than during the 2008-09 recession. In Edmonton, Calgary, and Montreal, supply/demand weightings still favour sellers.

In the first 18 days of February, the sales-to-new listing ratio in Toronto was .31, lower than in 2009 (shown below since 2006). Anyone looking to buy in areas with low sales-to-new listing ratios should not be afraid to make lowball offers and be patient as sellers become more realistic, aka ‘motivated.’

In the 2008 global recession, Canada fared better than most countries because it entered the downturn with relatively less inflated home prices and lower levels of household debt.

Unfortunately, what were tailwinds then have reversed into headwinds for Canada in 2025 (Canada’s household debt to GDP shown below since 2005).

The unnatural jump in Canada’s residential property prices from 2008-2022 is shown below (chart since 1970). Previous (lesser) overshoots have been corrected via sharp drops/years of flat prices. This time is unlikely to be different.

Canadians need a new national obsession and investment focus outside counter-productive housing speculation. It’s time to invest in innovation and productivity enhancements that lower costs and improve life quality. MCGA (make Canada great again)?

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Joby Aviation: Priced For Perfect Execution
data.description:
Since our last review of Joby Aviation (NYSE:JOBY), one of the leading companies at the forefront of eVTOL development, the stock has performed quite worse than the S&P 500 (SPY), with a return of only 5.19% versus 34.98% for the S&P 500. In that article, we showed our skepticism and gave Joby a hold rating because we thought the path to commercialization might be more unclear than investors expected.

About 1.5 years later, Joby has made tremendous progress in the interim, both in terms of achieving type certification for their aircraft and international developments in markets such as the UAE, South Korea and Japan that we discuss in this article. At a valuation of $6BN, however, we are concerned that Joby's price for Type Certification is almost a certainty and that much operational success is likely priced into our updated valuation model.

Working Towards Commercialization

In a way, Joby has recently begun to refute many of the naysayers who label eVTOL as a pipe dream, as much progress has been made toward FAA certification, and it looks more and more like commercialization can become a reality. Overall, Joby still needs to get type certification to fly commercially in the United States.

According to the latest earnings report, the company is just about done with the first 3 stages of certification, including the certification basis, the means of compliance and the submission of certification plans. The FAA is reportedly now through 21% of the Test & Analysis stage, while Joby is around 41% and has also made progress for stage 5. In terms of specific developments, Joby reported that it has tested a major aircraft structure for the first time with the FAA, completing static load testing for the tail structure.

In addition to completing static load testing for the tail structure, management also now claims that approximately 35% of composite parts made at Joby are built for FAA-compliant use in TIA aircraft and tested for credit, which is a remarkable achievement. In addition, Joby continues to make tremendous progress in international markets, with flights in both South Korea and Japan. In South Korea, for example, the company participated in the K-UAM Grand Challenge and became the first company to complete flights under the program.

It was in Japan not too long ago where Joby made its first international test flight outside the United States, at the Higashi-Fuji Technical Center in Shizuoka as a result of their long-standing partnership with Toyota (TM). This partnership with Toyota was seemingly strengthened in October, when the company announced an additional $500M investment in Joby in cash for common stock, with a $250M tranche well underway and another $250M tranche expected to close this year, depending on terms. This additional liquidity, along with $222M raised through a follow-on offering and the $710M in short-term cash, should bring Joby's total liquidity position to an estimated $1.4BN, virtually its strongest position since the IPO.

In another market, the Middle East, Joby has begun construction on one of 4 expected initial vertiports in the UAE. The first is the Dubai International Airport, where Joby plans to launch its services in late 2025 at the earliest. Although we think this may be optimistic and will probably take place sometime in 2026, given that Joby's timelines have proven to be rather optimistic in the past. Additionally, Joby is also working with Mukamalah, a subsidiary of the largest oil producer Saudi Aramco, to bring operations to Saudi Arabia, as they reportedly operate the world's largest fleet of corporate aircraft.

Perhaps the most significant regulatory development we have seen recently in the United States is the finalization of the new SFAR or Special Federal Aviation Regulation that was released for powered elevator and pilot certification. This is the first time in 80 years that a new category has been released for civil use since helicopters in the 1940s, which we believe indicates that the FAA is serious about marketing eVTOL. This final version of the SFAR has also addressed some concerns for Joby and other eVTOL makers when it comes to pilot training and energy reserves, which were considered too conservative in the original SFAR by leading developers in the space.

On pilot training, the SFAR includes a clear pathway for us to train pilots using the high-fidelity flight simulators we are qualifying alongside CAE and a single set of pilot controls. The rule also confirms our expectation that the energy reserves required in service will be equivalent to helicopter operations under VFR rules today. (Q3 Earnings Call)

Priced For Perfection

And yet, despite the significant progress being made, we are reluctant to have Joby as a Buy, which is mostly due to valuation concerns we have. JPMorgan also pointed this out recently, saying that the “hype may be too high” and that they think they are “acting as if type certification has already been successfully completed.”

Fundamentally, for a company worth $5.99BN, Joby is virtually still a company with almost zero revenue whose valuation depends entirely on theoretical future results. And it may be some time before the company trades at a reasonable valuation, given LTM operating income is -$575.12M with a free cash flow of -$432.18M for the same period. Overall, at a multiple of 18x, Joby should yield $314.44M annually, which is a $746.62M gap compared to where free cash flow currently stands.

The problem we see primarily is the fact that at a valuation of about $6BN, certification is more or less priced in, in our opinion, as is a certain degree of certainty that Joby will become a viable business model and become profitable at a fairly rapid pace. From a financing perspective, we think Joby is strongly positioned with $710M in cash and short-term investments, which combined with $222M additional raised through offerings and $500M from the Toyota investment should bring their balance sheet to $1.4BN.

Management expects FY2024 to have a cash outlay of $440M to $470M, which is likely to be closer to the lower end of the guided range, meaning that at the current burn rate Joby has just over 3 years of cash left. We think this should be enough to pass certification, although we still see the possibility of additional dilution to get through the early stages of scaling up production. In making our valuation, we also encountered the difficulty of not having details of what the ramp-up will look like over the long term and what unit profitability will ultimately look like, as they are mostly speculative. The only guidance we really have were estimates put out at the time of Joby's IPO through SPAC, which initially assumed a 2024 launch and cash flow profitability by the end of 2025, targets that in retrospect were far too optimistic.

Nonetheless, we decided to chart an optimistic scenario of what a successful ramp and certification could look like through 2034, as we see value being realized mainly in the very long term. In our optimistic estimates, we see Joby operating with over 2045 eVTOL by 2034 and generating $4.5BN in revenue, with each eVTOL generating $2.1M per year, as previously estimated by management.

As for cash burn, we see Joby becoming cash flow positive when the threshold of 400 operational eVTOL is crossed, which in an optimistic scenario would be in 2028. While the ramp-up may seem steep in the first few years, we believe Joby can meet these goals with Toyota as an operating partner in manufacturing.

Although management believes in their SPAC presentation that they can generate more than 40% adjusted EBITDA margins, we think this is probably too optimistic, like most other forecasts made a few years ago, and we have calculated that free cash flow margins will grow to 20% by 2034, which should still be above the average in the airline/ride-sharing sector. As for the discount factor in the DCF model, we came up with 12.58%, which is mainly based on a cost of equity of 12.68%, since Joby has very little long-term debt. This cost of equity includes a beta of 2.33, a risk-free interest rate of 4.48% based on the 10-year Treasury, and an expected return of 8%.

Applying a reasonable growth rate of 3%, we are left with $9.67BN, which after discounting amounts to $2.96BN. Adjusting for incoming discounted cash flows during the period and net debt, we are left with $4.56BN of equity value, or $5.96 per share. This is well below the $7.83 per share the company is currently trading at.

Moreover, there are many risks associated with this optimistic statement, even if Joby can achieve type certification somewhere later this year or in the near future. For example, the eVTOL market itself is expected to grow to $4.67BN by 2030, according to market research, at which point we estimate Joby's revenues to be $2.1BN, meaning they would represent nearly 45% of the entire market.

Perhaps the biggest risks lie in the fact that the demand outlook is uncertain, as these eVTOLs have yet to reach the commercial market on a large scale. Especially with Joby, given that they are going for the direct route to consumers by offering an air taxi service themselves, compared to its closest competitor Archer Aviation (ACHR) which plans to sell eVTOL directly and already has an order book of more than $6 billion. Even compared to the traditional helicopter market, Joby would be one of the largest aircraft manufacturers in 10 years with more than 2,000 aircraft, as just over 9,300 commercial helicopters were in operation in the United States in 2019 alone.

And while the prospects for eVTOL could be significant in terms of competition for traditional ride-sharing on the ground with cost improvements in fuel and an improved level of autonomy, there are operators such as Blade (BLDE) that offer similar services for low-cost short-haul flights, but generate only $241.81M on a TTM basis, are on track to have sold more than 155K seats in 2024 and still have negative operating income.

The Bottom Line

Although Joby is getting closer to type certification of its aircraft and has made significant progress in terms of their liquidity, estimated at $1.4 billion, we believe the company needs near-perfect execution to live up to its current valuation.

We believe that not only will Joby likely be priced for successful type certification, but also that the company will be able to ramp up to over 2,000 operating eVTOL over the next 10 years while generating an impressive 20% free cash flow margin over the long term. Even if it were able to live up to these estimates, we see the fair value being more likely to be around $5.96 per share, which is a downward drop of 24% from where the stock is currently trading. As things stand, with the current valuation, we believe there are too many operational and execution risks to warrant a buy rating.

Seeking Alpha's Quant currently has Joby as “Buy,” with the positive factors being profitability and momentum, while valuation, growth and earnings revisions remain a drag on the rating.

data.title: Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals: Progress During Adverse Market Conditions
data.description:
Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:ARWR) reported fiscal Q1 2025 results and provided a pipeline update last week. The good news was that zodasiran is back in the clinic for the treatment of a rare disease called HoFH, and that we may see data updates from up to three programs partnered in late 2024 with Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT) – ARO-DM1, ARO-DUX4, and ARO-MMP7.

The bad news, well, there was no bad news from the company, but the problem in the near-term is that Arrowhead is still lumped into a group of genetic stocks that are being relentlessly sold for an extended period of time, and the stock is down approximately 15% since my last public article in May 2024. As such, I am not terribly optimistic about sentiment changing for the stock in the near-term if it does not change for the group.

But it is also not impossible to break away from the group, and a powerful catalyst (or two) could change the sentiment. One standout performer in the group is BioNTech (BNTX), which performed well in the last 12 months due to company-specific updates from its oncology pipeline – it has one of the more advanced clinical candidates in a hot area – a PD-L1/VEGF antibody.

In today’s article, I will provide updated thoughts following the financial results and the pipeline update.

Zodasiran is back in the clinic

Of all the programs that were announced as non-core last year, I was most disappointed to see zodasiran's de-prioritization. The company is still reluctant to do cardiovascular outcomes trials of zodasiran and/or plozasiran, but I believe that a small phase 3 trial in homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (‘HoFH’) is a no-brainer. The cost of the trial will be modest, the indication is de-risked with a shared target with Regeneron’s (REGN) Evkeeza, and Evkeeza is showing a path to zodasiran adding $200 million+ to Arrowhead’s topline at peak and more than $100 million to the bottom line due to the significant operating leverage through the use of the plozasiran sales force.

Evkeeza generated $125.7 million in net sales in 2024 with 60% Y/Y growth in each of the four quarters of 2024. And zodasiran is well-positioned to compete here with what should be similar efficacy, and convenient quarterly subcutaneous injections versus once-monthly IV infusions of Evkeeza.

Potential updates from three Sarepta-partnered programs

Arrowhead continues to enroll the phase 1/2 trials of ARO-DM1, ARO-DUX4, and ARO-MMP7, and since late 2025, these programs are partnered with Sarepta Therapeutics.

Arrowhead's management said on last week's earnings call that we may see data from all three trials later this year, but that disclosures are subject to agreement with Sarepta. If these trials are generating good data, I am sure Sarepta will waste little time and that it will allow Arrowhead to provide data updates.

Sarepta willing to pay $300 million in milestone payments for enrollment targets in the ARO-DM1 trial is unusual and the trial is enrolling up to 48 patients. This “smells” like registrational intent on Sarepta’s side, but we still need to see positive data from the trial for that to happen.

The other two candidates could also rapidly progress to registrational trials, and while I am not optimistic on ARO-MMP7 for the idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, it is interesting that Sarepta wanted this program, so, there might be an efficacy signal in this early trial as well.

Plozasiran will be ready for launch in late 2025, SHTG trials to complete enrollment in mid-2025

The Breakthrough Therapy Designation was of no help to plozasiran – the FDA gave a 12-month review period for plozasiran for the treatment of familial chylomicronemia syndrome (‘FCS’). The approval of Ionis Pharmaceuticals’ (IONS) Tryngolza (olezarsen) is to blame here since it addresses the unmet need for this population, and makes the FDA less interested in a fast review of a second product for the same condition.

Plozasiran has either met or exceeded the expectations I laid out in May 2024 article. It delivered significant triglyceride reductions in the phase 3 trial in FCS patients and it demonstrated a statistically significant reduction in acute pancreatitis events.

While we wait for the PDUFA date for plozasiran, we should get three quarterly sales updates from Ionis which will show how the demand looks like in this very small market.

However, I want to note that this indication’s importance will be short-lived for both companies.

Ionis will complete the phase 3 trials of olezarsen in severe hypertriglyceridemia patients (‘SHTG’) in late 2025 and it will likely launch it in the first half of 2027, and Arrowhead is slightly behind as it will complete enrollment of plozasiran in SHTG patients in mid-2025 and it will likely receive approval and launch plozasiran for the treatment of this population in the second half of 2027. This means that the price of plozasiran and olezarsen will fall by a factor of, probably 25 or 30, and whatever FCS sales there are will be divided by that number – if the annualized net sales run rate is $100 million, it will go down to $3-4 million and if it is $300 million, it will go down to $10-12 million.

I will reiterate again that the long-term value of the FCS indication is very low, and that the SHTG indication is far more important and should unlock a significantly larger market than the FCS market will ever be even at the current price of Tryngolza of more than half a million dollars per patient per year.

ARO-INHBE and ARO-ALK7 to produce proof-of-concept data in obese patients 2H 2025 and 1H 2026

The ARO-INHBE trial started dosing obese volunteers in late 2024 and Arrowhead expects to have preliminary data from the single-ascending dose part of the trial in the second half of the year. And single doses should be more than sufficient to provide proof-of-concept – the weight loss effect and the impact on body composition, thanks to the long durability of effect of the drug.

ARO-ALK7 will start dosing obese volunteers in mid-2025 and this suggests we will see the preliminary data in the first half of 2026. As a reminder, this is Arrowhead’s first adipose candidate, and positive proof-of-concept data could lead to expansion of this side of the platform.

New core programs are coming

The cardiometabolic pipeline will get a new candidate this year – the CTA filing for the first dimer (a candidate reducing the expression of two target genes) is expected later this year and the targets are APOC3 and PSCK9. This should be a very powerful cardiovascular drug, basically a combination of plozasiran and Novartis' (NVS) and Alnylam's (ALNY) Leqvio (inclisiran), as it should significantly reduce triglycerides, remnant cholesterol, and LDL cholesterol.

The CNS pipeline is progressing as well, and while some of the candidates were given to Sarepta, there are other candidates that Arrowhead still wholly owns. CTAs for ARO-HTT for the treatment of Huntington’s disease (this one is partnered with Sarepta) and ARO-MAPT for the treatment of Alzheimer’s disease are expected toward the end of the year and the CTA for ARO-SNCA for the treatment of Parkinson’s disease is expected in 2026.

The company has still not determined whether CNS will be one of the core platforms and the decisions will be data-driven. Management’s current thinking is to keep ARO-MAPT in-house and to try and find a partner for ARO-SNCA.

Other non-core programs are waiting for partners

The rest of the pipeline is now non-core and will not progress without partners – ARO-RAGE, ARO-C3, ARO-CFB, ARO-PNPLA3. I am not really optimistic about the partnering possibilities for these candidates, but would like to see ARO-CFB and ARO-C3 developed for complement-mediated diseases.

Financial overview – Arrowhead is in the best financial shape ever

Arrowhead ended calendar 2024 with $553 million in cash and equivalents. The deal with Sarepta closed last week and it will add $825 million to the company’s balance sheet for a pro-forma cash position of $1.38 billion. Arrowhead is also entitled to receive $50 million a year from Sarepta for the next five years, and to another $300 million in near-term milestone payments that are tied to enrollment targets in the phase 1/2 trial of ARO-DM1 which the company expects to hit in the following quarters.

In the next two years, Arrowhead expects the operating cash burn to be in the $500-550 million range, and that the cash balance will be sufficient to fund the company into 2028. The graph below shows how the cash position evolution should look like in the next five quarters, and this includes $350 million in additional payments from Sarepta in calendar Q4 2025 and Q1 2026.

Arrowhead’s cash position can further improve in the following quarters and years from additional milestone payments from Sarepta, other existing partners, new partners, plozasiran revenues or royalty-backed deals.

The times when we had to think about where the next cash injection will come from are behind us, but management is still reluctant to run some studies or clinical programs – primarily the cardiovascular outcomes trial of plozasiran.

Conclusion

I am not very optimistic about the near-term share price performance due to the negative biotech sentiment and even worse sentiment in Arrowhead’s area of genetic stocks, but the fundamentals continue to improve and the cash injection from Sarepta and upcoming milestone payments allow the company to focus on pipeline execution and partnering discussions with far less pressure and urgency.

I am looking forward to the updates from several programs in the following quarters, most of all from ARO-INHBE and ARO-DM1 trials this year, and from ARO-ALK7's trial next year.
